speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Edward Swenson took place at his daughter's home on American Ridge on August 10, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh. Raised them. Yeah. Well, we had a little variety of that over there.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: There was one family that was very, very strict with their children and partial. This man had married a woman that... No, wait a minute.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: This man had two children, a boy and a girl, when he married the second time. Then they had children together. And these two, this boy and a girl, they were not treated as good as the rest of the children.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The first family.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I can't understand why, why that man was... He was a little bit radical in his ways.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And his wife was pretty strict. And he took her side against his own children, you might say.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Because he seemed to think more of the children he had with his second wife than those two first ones.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had he and his first wife gotten along pretty good when they were together?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: We don't know because he had remarried this second time before we came to Idaho. He was there ahead of us and our in -laws. My oldest brother married into the Hellred family. And this Hellred family was married into this other just a little. One of the Hellred men married a sister to the wife of this man that was so strict. And they were neighbors.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A sister to his second wife?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So how would he take out his being partial? How would he show that?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, he... Now this boy that he had was the oldest of the family. Well, his two children were older than the children he had with his second wife, you know. And so that there was no other boy to show partiality to. Because after he married the second time, they had...

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: One, two...

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: They had three girls before they had a boy.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So far as I know, he lives in Clarkson now, that boy. So that his own boy was half grown up before he had a brother there. And so he had to carry the brunt of the old man's vengeance when he got mad at him, you know. And he was alone. There was no other boys.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And they... The way he talked to him... That oldest boy of his was the same age as I was. Just a few months difference in our age. So we were together a lot, you know. And I can remember one day, they had a well down in Lure, kind of a creek run through, you know. And it was a little downhill to that. And they had a well there and they had a well wheeled up on it. And they would run the bucket down, you know, and pull the water up. And this boy and his sister, his full sister, they had to carry water from there up to the house. They had a one by four, about six feet long. And they would run that through the handle of two five gallon coal oil cans. And they'd get that on their shoulder, you see, and carry it up to the house. And one day this boy had forgotten to replace this bucket down at the well. So when his father and my father were working, my father was helping him. And he came to the well and the bucket wasn't there. And he turned around and he says to that boy, he said, if you ever leave that bucket away from here again, he says, I'm going to whip you till the blood runs down your back. That was the language he'd use. I got so mad at that man, I was just a kid. But from then on I hated that man. And one day the stepmother to this boy, I don't know what was wrong there at the house, but she picked up a stove poker and she just put his skin wide open around his head. My older brother and I, we were down there and he had his hat on and he says, come along, we went down to the barn, I want to show you something. And he took his hat off and there was blood all in his hair there, you know, and that scalp open. That's the way they treated him. And this man tried to run the neighborhood too, but he went stuck there. He carried a long barreled six -shooter on his hip there the first few years. But he never had a chance to use it. One day he drew it and his brother -in -law took it away from him. And after that, he kind of quieted down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This friend of yours who got bad treatment, was he expected to do an awful lot of work around the house too?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, of course he did.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: They had several milk cows in those times. And this boy and his sister, they had to help with the milking. And they kept a lot of hogs and they had to carry milk and stuff to the hogs, you know. And the boy had to, the cows would graze around on the valley there, you know, when they had to gather them up at night, rain or shine.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Their first house burnt to the ground when he was...

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: when the boy was, I can tell you fairly close, because we had moved into our new house and that was in 1901. So I was...

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I was about 16 or 17 years old and that boy would be the same. While he got a bed, some blankets and stuff in the loft where the woodshed that he slept in. So he didn't sleep in the house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In the new house?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah, in their new house. They had two bedrooms upstairs and one bedroom downstairs. So there would have been room, but the girls, they could have more freedom there, you know. And his sister had moved away by that time. She went, I think it was to Seattle, went to some people there and worked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Worked as an aide in their home? Worked in their home.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So she never came back except she came once to visit a little while. So it was just that boy then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think he thought of the way he was treated? The what? What do you think he thought of the way he was treated?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: The boy?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, I don't know. He didn't take it very serious. He took it almost like if he was a slave. He was afraid to say anything, you know, until he grew up. When he grew up, he told his father often that he left home. He got to working with a threshing outfit and working in the cookhouse. And he took that up as a business. He had a restaurant in Elk River town after that. He cooked at some of the camps. You know, they were logging around Derry and up in there. And he cooked at the camps and then he settled in Elk River and he was at the head of that. He was doing okay. And he was a pretty decent man. I saw him different times after he was full grown.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he stop having anything to do with his father?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh yeah. Once I was in Elk River, my God, that boy's dad came there to the restaurant for supper in the evening. And the boy was civil to him. He didn't just treat him like anybody else. But that was all.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, his father died not so long after that. It was an accident. First, his father hauling stuff to Elk River. And they had a railroad running in there, you know. And one part through the woods there, the railroad came up fairly close to the road. And his team ran away and threw him out and broke his neck. That's a fact. It broke his neck. And they took him on a... They didn't have doctors in Elk River that could do anything. So they had a log on training there. And they took him to Bovell and then he was sent to Spokane. And it was such an unusual thing, I heard about it, that special doctors came clear to Spokane to see this condition. They had to turn his head around. He told me afterwards, he said he could look down his back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he recovered from that?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And he recovered.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I saw him after he came from Canada. He was still there at the park.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But then what killed him?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, later on, he fell into a scalding vat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For hogs?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah. And that finished him. He lived a few days even then. But he was scalded so bad, I heard them say that the flesh loosened from the bones. In part of his body. But that killed him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how he could have fallen in?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, I wasn't there, but you know a scalding vat is up off the ground just a little bit. And we used to have that too. You have a little bit of a platform then, full length of that. And you put ropes around the hog and rolled him into the scalding vat, and then you pulled him back with the ropes. And I don't know somehow whether he got tangled in the ropes or what, but he fell into the trough there.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: That was up on Round Meadow, if you know where that is. And they had to take him to Elk River before they had the doctor for him. They had to take him in there. And that's where he was shipped then to Spokane.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would a bunch of farmers get together at once to scald?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, there wasn't many in there on Round Meadow, you know, but he had some help there. I helped him there one spring with a spring work after we came back from Canada. And they had hogs there then, but we didn't do any butchery.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This man, would he have been considered fairly well to do at that time?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, speaking of how the times were then, he sold his place at Park, and he had this Round Meadow. He was in partnership with some men that had some money, and he was running this. Whether he finally bought it or not, I don't know. But he sold his place at Park right after we came back from Canada, and he got $5 ,000 for that. And at that time, that was lots of money, you know. So they were kind of semi -retired over there at Round Meadow. And well, then we moved away before he had that accident. So I don't know just what crowd he had, what conditions were, but that's where he had that accident.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm thinking more like, did they have enough money to have girls working for them? Staying at their house and working for them?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, his wife at that time was quite capable to do their own housework. And two of their daughters were here in Moscow at that time. And after he died, she moved and stayed with her daughters there in Moscow. And she was a strong pioneer woman, you know. I stayed with them when I helped them there. That was in 1926, I think it was. We moved back in 1925, and it was right after that. It would be about 1926. And she was a strong, healthy -looking woman then. Fairly big woman. And she was stronger in proportion than he was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In most families, do you think that the parents were fairly strict with their kids? Or how were they strict?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, I can tell you about my own parents. My mother was a very good, mild -mannered woman. Father was, he was kind of strict, but he never abused us, you know. Never did. But his word was law. So that, but of course, under the way times were and the way we were kind of all by ourselves in the park there, you know, we didn't get out much to town or to mix with other young folks very much. So we were kind of satisfied. We were all one kind of people in there, you might say. And so Father didn't have to be so strict. We didn't run away at night and things like that. And we had our little neighborhood dances in there and Christmas parties and that. We were satisfied. But if he, if I, when I got old enough to begin to think for myself about how to do things, and then he, he stopped quite a bit because he didn't understand what I was doing. I started to do a little carpenter work and things. And he didn't know the first thing about that, so he didn't try to say what I should do. But up until that, he would tell me what to do. And of course I did it. But he wasn't mean, you know, it wasn't that. He was cold sometimes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he expect you to do it the way he told you to, to do it his way?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, not exactly, because about the only thing he understood was ax work, timber work, you know, which is mostly what we were doing in those times. Fence posts and rails and stuff. We split rails and so to some of the neighbors there. And that was a lot of work. Then we cleared land and things like that. And of course then I worked for him, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would cause him to get, to get peeved at you? To get, what would set him off?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, if we, if we shirked, you know, or anything like that, he'd tell us to get busy. Or if, if we didn't do the chores up, he was right to the minute on chores, you know. And if we were a little slow on that, why, he'd tell us to get busy. And we didn't argue with him. But as we grew older, he began to kind of get, give us more freedom. So by the time I was fifteen, sixteen years old, I knew about as much about the work there as he did, so we worked together more. And gradually, well, he'd kind of turn it over to us. And of course my brother, older than I, he was four years older than I, so by the time I was half grown, he was full grown and out on his own. So then it was just my father and I. And they, as time went along, we got to be pretty good friends. He never, he never abused us really. And in a way, we were better off than some of our neighbours, because my parents were good, honest, respectable people. And that was one thing that he, both he and mother, told us about, that we should be like that. And they told us how we would succeed if we lived that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, they really tried to teach you to be honest and respectable?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh, they were right up to snuff on that. And they showed us that the way they lived.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And when I got old enough to go with father to town, when I go to Troy, that was before dairy was built, you know, he'd tell me a lot about things that he had to go through. And told me how he made out, you know. And that stuck to me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he feel that the towns or places like Troy were probably places that people should steer clear of and try to stay in the country?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: The only thing was that he didn't like the saloons, you know, although he took a drink once in a while himself. But he warned us about that, not to get mixed up in that. And they were religious, both father and mother, and they taught that to us.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And so, when I go to town with him, I would do the driving, you know, driving the horses. And when we get to town, I'd go and have a glass of beer or something right away. But I didn't go there. And then we'd do our training. We had plenty of time. We would get into town about ten o 'clock. We'd leave home about three in the morning. And we'd let the horses rest until about three or so in the afternoon to make the trip back home.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So, once in a while, he'd maybe have one or two minutes. But he was never cranky with me at that. No. So, we had no trouble there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When he, did he tell you that it had been, that life had been hard when he was growing up?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that part of the way he was telling you how to...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No. No, that was just forgotten. And he even began to ask me about this and that. How we should do it.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And he wasn't so strict that I felt angry with him over the years, you know. No. No, I kind of figured that it was about even up and everything okay. Yeah. And some of the neighbors there, they were a little too lenient with their kids. And I could see that somehow or other. I didn't, from the time I was quite young, I didn't think it was right for kids to sass their parents, you know. And then have to suffer for it.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So, my brother older than I, he took it a little harder than I did because he was a little more independent. But then as soon as he grew up, we didn't have trouble over it, not at least then. We kind of thought Dad was hard with us at times, but as we grew older, we could see that he had some reason for it. And of course, in those times, the parents were kind of strict with their kids, most of them. Now the Hellrudes, they had several children and I noticed that... Now there were two brothers that had families there, you see, Hellrude families. And one of them, the mother to the children, was a little too easy with them. And she would brag of them. The one Hellrude had a boy two or three years younger than I, and the other Hellrude had a boy same age as I. And he was a bigger boy than I was. And his mother used to be proud of telling about that once in a while. He could do more than I could, you know. And it kind of made him feel a little big until we were about half grown and we could figure things out together and we were good friends. We worked together and everything was okay. We never had any trouble.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well these parents that were more lax with their kids, did they let their kids get off without working around the house?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah, they didn't do as much work. They would work some, but... Like kid naughties. They had to kind of coax them more, you know.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Now this boy would mind his father fairly good. His father didn't let him talk to him. No, but he was more easy going on it. He didn't speak out quite as strict as my father did. You know, my father went through military training in Norway and that was kind of his life. And he was so precise about things. He even told me years later that he was given liberties in camp there. He had learned to butcher things and he got so that they let him kind of go out in the country and buy, like beefs, you know, bring in and do the slaughtering. And it kind of aroused him out when he didn't have to be in training. And he was so precise that when he finished there, he was given a silver watch.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: He used to say, if he had an appointment to be at a certain place, he used to say, it's better to be an hour early than to be a minute late.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And he got along good with his neighbors. If he was to help a neighbor, you could depend, he'd be there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about your mother? What about your mother? It sounds like she wasn't strict with you exactly and certainly not like your father was.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No, she was different.She never slapped us or anything like that. When she'd tell us we did wrong, we respected her. She was good and she was strong. We knew that she could handle us if she had to. Oh, she was strong. Yeah, when they lived in Minnesota, when they had grain to haul to town, she'd go out and help him, you know. They had, instead of the wheat sacks we have here, they had cotton bags. They were narrower and taller, longer. And she could take one and he'd take the other and throw it on the wagon. And, oh, she... And I remember one day one of our cows kicked her. She took a stick and she beat up on that cow and then she threw the stick over and she sat down and milked that cow.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I'll tell you.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah, but otherwise she was... I never saw her really angry with us kids. She would be kind of a little bit angry, but I mean she never used hard language. No, she didn't need to because we minded her.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And with the neighbors, if there was one woman in the country there that she didn't like, so she didn't go and visit her, but she never sat around and talked bad about her. She just left her alone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that a part of the life there for her to go visiting the neighbors?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No, she didn't go around very much. She was always busy, but people were always welcome to come there. The neighbors would come there, she always was friends with them. Even this woman that was so bad with her boy, she would come up to see her mother quite often. They were our nearest neighbor, about a quarter of a mile apart. And many a time that woman, especially when her husband was away, she'd come up and spend quite a while with Mother. Yeah, they were good friends. So Mother didn't... she didn't get angry with people. If they didn't do right, that wasn't her business. When they came there, they were friends to her. She welcomed any of them that would come there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You say she was really busy with work. Was work for her something she did all the time?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, we had a big garden and then we milked three or four cows at one time, and she helped with that. She did most of the garden work. I'd be with Father, you know. And she helped with the milking and all that, and did her own sowing and things like that, you know. So she was busy. And she was healthy. She was never to a doctor that I can remember, and Father the same way. You couldn't wish for better people for living way back in like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I understand that for some women that that was a very hard life and they didn't have very good health and trying to...

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, yes, there were a few, but most of them in there were pretty healthy people.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: This one of the hell roots, he lost his wife. She got sick and that was when I was quite young, so I don't even know what she was sick from. And it was a long ways to go and get a doctor, you know.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And this family that, where he married the second time, they lost two babies that I remember of, shortly after they were born. And they were buried out there a little ways from the house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you go to your mother if you wanted something like shoes or something special? Would she be the one you would ask for? For clothing or something?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh no, we could ask Father about that. We didn't have to ask. He furnished us. We were satisfied. He bought boots for us during the winter sometimes and when spring came they weren't wore out so we cut the tops off and used them as shoes, you know.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No, we were never suffering for that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I've heard in a lot of families, and I don't know if this would be true of yours and those you knew, but I heard a lot of families, the father, the mother would be affectionate with the kids, you know, she would show affection, and the father really didn't. He just didn't feel that was right, you know, and that the mother did. Is that pretty much the way it was with you?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: One of those families, it was a little that way, this one that bragged our boy, you know, a little that way, but not very bad. So far as I know, they worked together pretty good. And it almost had to be with pioneers like that, you know, they had to depend on each other pretty much and work together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have dances in park before you left?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: We had a few, yeah, we had a few.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you know, I was going to ask you what your parents taught you about religion.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, they were strict Lutherans, you know, so that was, and so were the neighbors there, nearly all of them. There was one neighbor that was Methodist, and he was a little better, you might say, educated about religion, so they chose him for Sunday school teacher. The others were Lutheran, he was Methodist, but he taught the Sunday school there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he Norwegian?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh yes, yeah. Yeah, so they weren't really radical about it, you know. It was more a case of being a good neighborhood.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did they think that, did your parents teach you that religion was supposed to be important in your life?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh yes, and of course, as we grew up, we had no occasion to consider or think about any other religion than the Lutheran. We didn't get out to mix with others very much. I can remember when we were confirmed, the minister gave us a little talk then, advice, you know. And he said, soon you will be out on your own, he said, among other people. And you will mix with people and hear their side of things, and in religion, he said, they will try to win you over. But don't listen to them. I thought to myself, don't worry, I'm not going to listen to them.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: But as I got out and began to get acquainted with others, I found they were good people too.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So, we never had any trouble with anybody over religion, not at all, because we began to understand that there was two sides to it, with most of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you tell me just a little bit of what you learned when you grew up and what the Lutheran teaching was?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: In what way?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, in how to live your life and how God was to people.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, we got that through our Sunday school, and then the minister, he would come in there once a month, and we had lessons to go through with him, you know. We would have in our books, we would have several pages then for that one time. So we practically had learned that so we could answer any question that he'd ask us. And that was what we believed in, and that was it.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And we didn't, in fact, we didn't have any chance to do much wrong because we were all under this one strict religion. And at that time everybody believed his neighbor was honest, and we never had any trouble about any tools or anything like that being missed, you know.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Our barns and houses and woodsheds and so on was open for anybody to go in if they wanted to. Nothing was ever missed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did people talk about religion among themselves and discuss it?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh, to some extent they did, but they didn't make much of an issue about it. They all believed the same way, and that was it.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So there was one Adventist family that lived in there. He neigbored with us. There was no trouble. And at one time an Adventist minister came in there to visit them, you know, and he had asked our school directors if he could have a meeting in the schoolhouse, and they gave him permission. So he came in to our Sunday school one Sunday. He came in to announce this. He came in and he sat down back by the door. The school was quiet and still Sunday school was over, and then he got up and asked permission to speak. And then he announced that he had been given permission to have services there the next Saturday, and he invited us to come. And I was looking at the people and at him when he said this, and I could see some of them were giving them pretty hard looks there. They figured he was a bad man, you know. And of course they didn't go to his services.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did anyone go besides that Adventist family?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I don't know. We didn't go, and I don't know about the others. I don't think many went there. You see, when people are drilled into believing one certain thing for a long time, they don't want to believe anything else. So they did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I was wondering just a little bit of what the Lutheran teaching was, what it taught you about religion.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, I don't see any big difference. The Adventists, of course, believed in Saturday being the holiday, and there were certain foods that they didn't think they should eat, and that was about it. And in what I have read and learned since those times, I think it was almost silly that they should argue about things which were not so important. Now, being Saturday being a holiday, that was almost more correct than having Sunday, because if you go into the Bible about it, Christ kept the seventh day, you know, and his disciples did. And I haven't found any place in the Bible where it says that they kept Sunday. I mean his disciples. They met on Sunday in remembrance of his rising back to life, you know. They kept that as a special day, but I don't say that they didn't keep the Sabbath. So there you are.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about dancing? I'd understood that some churches were against dancing. They didn't want them to dance.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: My parents were, and most of the neighbors there were. They had been brought up to believe that having dances and being jolly and all that, and to a great extent, they thought it was not right. And they didn't have a chance to look at both sides enough to. I remember when I was around 17, 18 years old, I got an accordion. I learned to play that some. That was okay, but later I got a violin, and my mother didn't think that that was good, because they used violins at dances. So I had my room upstairs, and that's where I'd play my violin, and I got so I could play some of the hymns we used in Sunday school. So I took the violin down, and I sat down and I played one of them to mother. That settled it. The violin was okay.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So you see, they would have one line of thought, and that carried through from their parents to them and on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But then when it reached your generation, it changed a little bit, because you saw, didn't you start seeing these other ways, because you must have had some reason to get a violin.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, I liked music, and I didn't consider any music instrument being different than another. So, you know, you get a little more open -minded as you grow up, you know, and it didn't matter to me as long as it was music. And dances, now, my parents didn't really, she didn't condemn dancing, but still she thought it wasn't really religious, you know. But when we grew up and we began to go dances, dances, well, they didn't object to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have dances at park, or did you go most of the other places to dance?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh, we had dances around home. You know, in the wintertime sometimes roads would be pretty bad, and we didn't get outside. We supplied up with groceries for the winter, and we were back there through the winter. But, now like this man I told you about, married the second time, he had the biggest house in the district, so we had dances there.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And we were friends, we young folks were, we didn't regard anything like the old people did, you know, about things. So we were good friends, and we had dances together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where do you think the young folks got the idea that it was all right to dance if the parents would...

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, first, naturally, couldn't see anything wrong with it, you know. Now, if anybody got drunk at a dance, we didn't stand for that. No, no. That would be wrong. Or, if a fella got to flirting too much with some of the girls, we didn't think that was right. So, they didn't get away with something like that. So it was quite peaceful, and okay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have get -togethers at homes that were just parties and not dances?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah. Sometime after Sunday school, the neighbor would have the rest of them invited to come to their place, you know. And maybe another time over to some other place, and so on. We had good visits together. Of course, we boys, if that was in the summer, we weren't in the house much, you know. We'd be out either chasing squirrels or something, and yeah. They'd put up good dinners, you know. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you play party games?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, we started playing cards a little bit, but... And then there were other games that parents had taught the children, you know. Indoor games. I don't remember much of it now. There wasn't so much of that. There were some games where we... I remember one game that you had to forfeit. If you missed the answer, you know, you had to forfeit something. I got stuck in one of them. I was kind of going with a girl, but it wasn't very serious, but at the same time, she looked better to me than the rest. At this party, I didn't get the right answer, and so the penalty was... We were lined up there, kind of. You should start in there. The girls were all by themselves here, and the boys were all... So then you were to go to these, and you should bow to the wittiest, and kneel to the prettiest, and kiss the one you loved best. Boy, was I in the corner, and I was kind of bashful too in those times.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Something turned up, and my girl was there. I would rather take a licking than go over and kiss her, you know.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And something turned up before I got started on that. I don't know what it was, but it broke up the game. That saved my life that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, would you have kissed her if you had to?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I suppose I would have had to. I thought first that I'd kneel to her as the prettiest, and kiss one of the other girls. But I didn't get to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Boy, that's a mean one, isn't it?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, that was a rough one.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: I remember that when I was a kid. I'd do that. They would have teached the life out of me afterwards, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It sounds like games like that were a chance to get to pair off, and get to know the girls.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: One game, they'd get pair off sometimes. One game was that you were to go into another room, and you were not told what was going to happen. You were going to go in another room, and they fixed up two chairs, and they strung a blanket over there real tight. And two girls would sit down on those chairs, and there was a space between those chairs. And underneath that blanket was a tub full of water, or a part full of water. And it was packed like this, and the blanket would go down to the floor, across from those chairs. So when you came in, you didn't know about this. And then you were supposed to come in, and you were supposed to talk to those ladies, say hello and so on. And then they would ask you to sit down, you see, between them. That put you right in the tub, you know. Because when you sat down, they'd get up, you know, in the blanket, and you would go into the tub. I didn't get caught in that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think that the girls were raised different, any different than the boys, as far as, like, the boys having more freedom, they could go around and do more than the girls?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes, of course, the boys would. We could go from one place to another, but not very many of the girls would go alone. They would come, like when my younger sister was home, or there'd be other girls come to visit her. And sometimes then they'd go to another place, you know, together. But not as much as the boys would. But there were very few of us boys old enough to really pick out a special girl to go with. And if he did, why, he better stay home pretty well, because the whole neighborhood would find out, you know, and they'd just teach her to death. So, there wasn't much of that going on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, if you wanted to go out with a girl, where could you take her anyway?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, that's it. There was no where to go, unless you want to go to some other neighbor with her. So, there wasn't much of that done.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And we didn't have any buggies. We had just wagons, you know, until the very last part. So, you couldn't take a girl out to town or anything like that. One Christmas, a bunch of us young folks got together. I furnished a team of horses, and the neighbor furnished a bobsled. And we all got into that and went clear down to what was called Nora, down there, to her Christmas doing. We left right after noon, got down there in time for the doings, and then back home at night. And we got caught in a snowstorm going back home. So, before we started out, why, there was four, no, there was three of us boys and four girls. And we arranged that, and one should drive all the way coming down, going down. And going back at night, the other two should share the driving. And I drove all the way going down. So, the other two had to set up there in the snowstorm and drive the horses. I got, and my girl was in that party. We had a lot of blankets with us, and we pulled a blanket over us, so we were in the dry. And of course, the other girls, they'd sit there and kick at us and tease us, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, does that mean, it sounds to me kind of like that most things were done in groups, and if a group of boys and a group of girls. I suppose that if a boy and a girl went off by themselves, then it was pretty serious. If they, for instance, if they went into town just the two of them together or something like that, that would be more serious.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah, it could have been. If we'd had a way of going, I suppose we would have. But the girls didn't do much horseback riding, for one thing. The boys couldn't. We had horses we'd ride, you know, but the girls wouldn't. So, they didn't get out of the neighborhood except to go to town for shopping. So, we spent the winters pretty much right back in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the girls expected to work as hard as the boys?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh, no. No. They helped with the housework. They never did much work outside except in the garden and tend to the chickens and things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But they worked?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh, yeah. And they were all strong and healthy, so they could do heavy work, but we never had them out with farm work.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, this outlaw I speak about, he had the older girls out helping to shock the grain and helping the hay. But they were big, strong girls, so they could do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But that was kind of unusual?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah, they were about the only ones that had to go out. And when they got the older way, then the older ones would get out to work, you know, and then the younger boys grew up. So, they didn't have to do much of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that your generation, the boys that were growing up when you were, do you think that they believed that there was a really good chance to get ahead?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And, you know, they talk about making a lot of money. In those days, people thought they could make a lot of money?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No, we didn't think much of that. We thought more about getting a good job of some kind that we would earn money at. And speaking for myself, I never thought about getting rich or anything like that. I never did. I was more anxious to get a, what I first liked to have had was an education. But as conditions were, I was there with my parents, and of course I had to stay with them, you know. So the next thing was to learn a trade, and I liked carpenter work. So I began to kind of work towards that, together with the farming. And I liked farm work too, but I kind of liked to do more particular work, like carpenter work is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you get a chance to learn carpentry?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: My older brother farmed about 30, 35 miles from us, and I was over there once, and he had a horse I liked better than mine, and I talked him into making a trade. You gave him the horse, and you gave him... I was to give him $20 to boot on horses. And I didn't have the money, but he needed my help. He had a carpenter there, and he was just starting to build a barn on his place, a big barn. And he needed someone to help this carpenter. And I liked that kind of work. I had done just a little of it before, so I got to work with him. Those 20 days, I got a dollar a day, you know. And he was a very good man to work with. He could take time to show me how to do things to do it right. So by the time we got through with that, I could do some carpenter work. And then back home, we had a little bit to finish on the house. And I did that, and then we built a barn, and I did that. And then I did a little work for some of the neighbors. I worked on a church that we built, volunteer work, some, and things like that. And then eventually I moved over to where my brother lived, and farmed there. And I did some work there. I built a belfry on a schoolhouse there, and things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you learn most of it on your own, or did you learn most of it from other people watching?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No, I worked on my own.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: After I got started, it seemed to kind of come natural for me to do this kind of work. And of course, I studied it as I went along, and didn't try to do more than I understood to do. I went on that way. And from then on, through life, I built a kitchen onto the house we had on the farm we bought. So I made a little money there, selling that. And added on to the barn, and things. And I built an addition to my brother's house. So every time I worked on a job, I learned a little, you know, through trial and error.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, I was going to ask you how far the school did go at Park. Did it go all the way up to the eighth grade?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No. No, the children didn't stay home long enough for that. Of course, they may have done after we left. You know, they built a new schoolhouse, and they may have gone further. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far did it go for most kids?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, I got through the fourth grade. And by that time, my older brother was leaving home, and got married. So I had to help Father, you know. At that time, you weren't supposed to go a certain grade, you know. When you got old enough to work, and you could add and subtract, you were through.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you then?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I was about fourteen years old. I was nine years old when I started school. And I was about fourteen when I went through about one grade a year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were in school, did you have to lay out much to help, to work, while you were going to school?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh, well, we had morning and evening, you know, and weekends. I worked at home. I never worked away from home in those days, except when harvest was on, where them neighbors got together and helped each other.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But I mean, did you ever have to stay out of school during the week to help?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No. No, not, you see, we had our school through the winter months. And at first we had only four months of school. So winter and early spring, then school was out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it didn't interfere with heavy work?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No. We had a small farm, so father managed most of that. And I got through in time for spring work, you know, and harvest.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, had there been any school where you lived before you were nine, before you came out here? Had there been a school?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No. I went to school a few days in Minnesota. I think it was a week. We left there in the spring, you know, and I went with my older brother to school. I can remember that, but I didn't, it wasn't long enough that I learned much. So I was practically a new beginner when we started in Idaho.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did they have the school when you first came here? No. It was a little later.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah, that's why I was seven and a half years old when we came, and father got a house built that summer cabin. So we moved into that. Well, then there was no schoolhouse, so then they had to build a schoolhouse next. And that took next summer to build that. That's why I was about nine when I started school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, I was going to ask you about when you left Park and you went to Central Ridge, is that because there wasn't enough land around that you could get to farm to make a living?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: We still didn't have, ours broke up. There was timber there, you know. And I rented a farm. The last three years we were at Park, I rented a farm from a bachelor neighbor. And I began to make a little money there, but I still couldn't see a good future ahead there. And I had a lot of ambition. And the neighbor of my brother over on the ridge there, he offered his place for sale.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So I talked to my brother about this place at Park. I got an idea he could afford to buy it maybe, so I put a price on it, $2 ,500. And we talked about it and he got kind of interested. I said we should keep that in the family. There was my father's homestead, where he spent a lot of his mother's life. And they would hate to see a stranger come in there on that place. So I suggested to him, I said, if you buy it, I can sell it to you on good terms. And that'll help me to pay for what I'm buying. So he decided he wouldn't. So he bought that place. And he paid down some and I had the more money to make a down payment on the place that I bought. So I went in debt, but it was good land there. So we made out okay. And that's why that place at Park is still, you might say, in the family. One of my nephews, my oldest brother's son has it now. So it stayed in the family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's Steiner?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah, that's him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now this brother of yours, your oldest brother, what was his name?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Swen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that's Helmer's father too, right?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah, that's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you went over to Central Ridge then and started to farm there.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah, we farmed there about, I think it was about five years. Yeah, I rented it out for two years. I got the idea I wanted to go to Canada and raise cattle, you know. There were a lot of that done up there before my time. And I thought there was still a place to get there. So when I had a chance to sell, I sold mine. My folks were living with my oldest brother there, you know. And so that was okay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But at Central Ridge, why did you wind up deciding that that wasn't worth the effort?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, farming was okay and I was making money enough to pay out my contract on there. But I had this idea that I could make money faster by having a cattle ranch. Up there, you know, you could lease land from the government if you took a homestead. But you had to raise hay to feed them about half of the year up in that north country, you know. I wasn't in a prairie country there. There could be big meadows and things and that's where you would get your hay. Well, I scoured all that pastry country and all that up in there and I couldn't find a place where there was enough hay for cattle. I even had a deal made for 40 head of these Black Angus cattle heifers, ready to go right in there. But I couldn't buy them until I had a place for them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where were you looking? What place was this you were looking for?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, I went north from Edmonton up in there, you know. I went east after that, but I went north up into what's called the Peace River country. And up there I met three cattlemen, two from Montana and one from southern Alberta that were looking for land. So the three of us, we went on a boat on the Peace River, clear way up into that north country there. And we finally found a place. There was a French Canadian man there that knew the country. And he had a team and wagon and he took us out there. That took us a day and a half to one way out in the wilderness. And there was a big prairie there, open land. And we all could get located there together. And not one of us decided to stay. For one thing, it was an awful road to get out there. In one place we ran into what they call a musk -ag. We had to unhook the horses and get to the land and run a cable back to pull the wagon through. And another thing, and of course you had to build, and the winters were long and hard. And I got to thinking, I kind of was getting towards getting married too at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would it be if you got out there and got sick?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Even the town we went out from was just a couple of stores and that was about it. And the steamboats went up there only twice a year. But we had heard about this wonderland, you know. But we didn't know about these hardships you might go through. So all of us went right back again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: None of you filed?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: None of us even. We went to, found a section corner. They had iron sticks for each section corner. And we stood there and looked around. And it wasn't much said for quite a while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Finally one of them turned to me and said, What do you say?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I said, No, you can have it. And the other said, No, we don't want it. So we went back and then I began to look for farmland then, with rangeland close by. I went east and towards Saskatchewan in there, different places. I found a place. I bought about a section of land there and then I was pasture land by it there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it difficult to find any place that wasn't taken?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah, it was. That is, that looked good.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: That restored harvest time and the crops looked good there. The price was good on wheat and grain. And you could buy. I bought one quarter section for $1250 from a land company. That was all raw land, of course. And I bought another quarter section, had some broken up on it, and had a crop on it. But he claimed the crop. I got that for $1600. Then there was a homestead to get right by there that was pasture land. And there was two springs on it and part of the Battle River run through one corner of it. I got that as a homestead. You could homestead without living on it if you had land joining it. And you had to put a certain number of cattle on there. And that would prove it up for you, which I did. And then there was another quarter section there that I didn't buy right away. And my brother came up there and said, He said, I'm going to buy that one. He lived in southern Almora. He bought that one. And he thought maybe his son would want to come up in there, you know. But he wouldn't go up there. So then later on I bought that. But the first two years we didn't get a thing. We lost the first year. I put in what I had on mine and I rented a place. And I found that. And when the grain was about waist high, it froze. It froze so I just wilted down. We got a little bit of hay out of it, but it just fell flat on the ground. It froze ice on the water outside there in a bucket I had. So you know it just...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What month of the year was that?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: That was in July. So then, and I had got married in the meantime. And so next year I went to work again at it. And that time we got enough so it froze again. But we could get hay out of it. And I sold one load of oats. That was all I got that year. A small load. And I had to buy seed grain again.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And we began to have children. That was a little extra expense, you know, and I had to build on to the house. Well that year, that third year, I had one load of wheat to sell.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And I'll tell you, that was hard times now.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So we began to talk about Idaho.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And I began to break up land as fast as I could afford it. So I had about 100 acres more in crops the last year I was there. And I figured out a way to sell. Nobody had money to buy wheat to mount to anything, you know. I had a neighbor there that wanted to buy it. So I figured out if I would sell almost on the same way as you rent the place, sell on crop payments.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because if you take a mortgage on a piece of land and he don't have a crop, what's he going to pay with?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So you're just as safe on crop payment as you are on the mortgage. And the contract was that if he failed when he had the crop, if he failed to pay, I could give him a certain length of time notice and I could repossess the land. So we sold. I sold to two different people there. And we moved back to Idaho.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many years were you up there?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, we were there from 1918 to 1925.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When it was hard times, after you lost those couple of crops, how difficult was it for you?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, you see, you couldn't ration anything else but grain there. We had enough, we had good hale land. We could cut what they call the buffer of grass. We cut that for hay. So I didn't have to buy hay for my horses and cattle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But what about, could you grow good gardens and that sort of thing there or not?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No, we could grow potatoes and carrots and cabbage, but it would be apt to freeze, you know. One year my potatoes froze down twice.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I tell you, it took a strong back to face up to it.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And I bought a few cattle to prove up that homestead. I paid up to $100 a head for cows, $50 for calves, and by the time we were ready to sell out, I had to sell what cattle I had for two cents a pound. Just imagine that.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Shipped them to Edmonton. A couple of us got together and shipped the carload lots, you know, and we sent the man up there to sell them. Is that because... I bought, during that time, just before I sold, I went to a sale and there was a nice big red cow there. I bought her for $15. A man came a little late to the sale. He came to buy that cow. And he was disappointed. So he offered me $20 and I sold the cow to him.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I paid $200 a head, no, I paid $750 for four horses. And when I sold them, I had 12 horses and I sold a bunch for $500 was all I got for them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to the market? What was going on? Was the price on everything just going down?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: You see, I came there the last year of the war, that is, I came there when I was settled in 1918. And the price was up. Wheat was $2 a bushel. I had to buy grain, seed grain, three years before I had the crop. And oats the same way was high. Horses and cattle was high. Then all at once, they began to go down. And it just kept going down. I heard about one cattle rancher that got in so deep that he just walked off and left everything. He left his herd of cattle and everything. He couldn't commence to break even, so he just left. Oh, I'll tell you, there was many of them that lived on almost nothing there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it get bad enough that you worried about having enough to eat?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, it was down pretty close. We had a little blackboard on the wall in the kitchen and the wife would write down what she needed because I'd go to town only once a week. And before I'd go to town, I'd sit down and write down on a piece of paper what I was going to buy. And we'd try to figure out what we could do without that was on the blackboard. And she was so good about saving things and not worrying about times. I worried more than she did. She was so good and she could make clothes for the children. They all were born there in that house. And so we got along.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: If she hadn't been as helpful as she was, it would have been rough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she used to that from her childhood to roughing it?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, pretty much. They lived very economically. But they lived good there. She was the only child. And her mother died the year before we were married. So her father came to live with us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She was a Bricca?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah, that's right.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So she had a lot of courage. Oh, you bet she did. Hard times. She did. But she was willing. Yeah. I had just a little two -room cabin when we married. But the next spring then we built on. Not only that, but we had to build a schoolhouse. There was no school there, you know. And some of my neighbors met at my place. That was the fall before I was married. To organize. And they chose me as one of the members. And then we had to get a contract with someone to build a schoolhouse. And there wasn't very many that had much of a house, but several of my neighbors had more house than we had. And they wouldn't board the carpenters. They came, some of them from, the main carpenter was from Edmonton and he hired some. Well, I was on the board. It was up to me and the others, you know, to see that they got a place to stay. One neighbor there had a nice two -story house built about the time I moved in there. And they wouldn't take them. Well, we had just this little place there and they came down to me. Said they had to get a place to stay or they couldn't build a school.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So I asked my wife, I said, and we had just a little 12 by 12 size kitchen.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And a bedroom. But this neighbor that had the big place, they had a cookhouse. And I figured if we could manage to board those fellows, I'd bring the cookhouse down and they could sleep there. So I asked my wife, yeah, she said she'd tackle it. There was, well, there was a couple of neighbors that helped, so there wasn't so many of them stayed, four or five, something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come these other neighbors didn't want to help? They had an interest in the schoolhouse too.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, they just said no. And you couldn't force them to keep them. You think that they... So we explained to the carpenters what the situation was and they said that's fine. We'll sleep in the cookhouse. I pulled that right up in front of our cabin there. So it was just a couple steps between them. And so she cooked for him. Steiner was up there. He helped him on there. He stayed with us and he slept up in the loft.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: He had almost crawled to get to bed there. He slept up there. So we made out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think these neighbors thought that these carpenters were just working people and they shouldn't help them because they were better than they were or something?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I don't know. I don't know and I didn't ask them. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How do you think that community up there compared to Park? Were they similar?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes. We had about three different nationalities there for neighbors. There was one or two English families and there was one...

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh, what were they?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: But they all spoke English. Of course, we were United States people. Oh yeah, there was one Scotch family.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: You see, there was two English families from different parts in England. And the two of them was on the board. The two of them and me. And then we had another one for Clark. But we got along real well. It was a good neighborhood. We got along real well. Exchanged work and things. In a new neighborhood like that, people need each other, you know. So they decide to get along together. A lot better than an old neighborhood, an old neighborhood might do. So we exchanged work and visited together. Run the school and had our Christmas doings and things. We never had any trouble.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you would say that it was really a lot like Park had been?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes, it was. Very much like that. There was one family that... They were not quite as honest as the others, but we didn't have any trouble with them. Because I made it understood with them myself the first fall I was there. He and I rode with him to town before I had bought any horses.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And he started talking about the neighborhood there. He said once, he said, You want to keep your house locked when you leave anywhere. His name was Ben. I said, Ben, I will never lock my doors against my neighbors. But I said, I keep pretty good track of what I've got. And wherever I find that, if it's missing, I said, either that man or I'll get the damnedest lickin' you ever heard tell of. He believed it. We got a long find.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: You don't want to put that on record. Oh, it doesn't matter.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: That's a good... Yeah, we got along. We joined land even. That horse that I had was right up to his place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think some of the other neighbors might have had trouble with him?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No, they steered clear of him and didn't have much to do with him. But I was next door to him, you know. I mean, we were a mile apart, our houses, but I joined land with him.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And so... ...our nearest town. And it's a good little town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far from Edmonton?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: It's about 120 some odd miles. I'm not sure of exact miles. Well, of course, they have different roads now, so there are more direct roads into it. So we didn't have to go to Edmonton for anything much. It sounds as though it was a lot rougher, though, to make a living up there than down here. It was because you couldn't have a variety of crops, you know. You couldn't have an orchard. And only the hardiest things for a garden.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: You couldn't even raise alfalfa there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No. No, just grain.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a lot of turnover of the people that lived there? A lot of people selling out?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Not during the time we were there. We had almost the same neighbors when we left there. They were pretty much the same.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: You see, they were mostly homesteaders, and that country hadn't been settled on when we came, so we were all new beginners, pretty much. Some were a few years ahead of us, but they were still breaking up land, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you left, where did you come? Did you come back to park?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I came back to park Idaho.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: We sold our land on crop payments, and of course we had to wait a while to try and get started here. And at that time, the old house at park was vacant. So I contacted my brother and asked him if I could move in. He said, sure. He said, you can move right in. And we stayed there a while, and then began to kind of move around to see what could be done. And they began to have crop failures again after we left, you know. One year, one of the buyers there, they were honest people, but when they didn't get a crop, they couldn't pay. And he was so near broke one year that all he could send me was $35. The interest alone at that time would have been $156.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: But he paid out every cent he could. That is my share of the crop. They were selling wheat one year there for 27 cents a bushel. So you can imagine. I don't know what he had to live on. It was just he and his wife on this lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that land mostly prairie, that land? Or was it timbered?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: It was kind of spotted. There was patches of poplar and willow and prairie. If you were in a plane, you could look down on it as just like a spotted cow or something. So to break up land, to mount anything, you had to clear those patches of poplar and willow and that stuff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So when you went back to park, did you just have to wait until you could do any farming?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I rented a place there for a year or two while we were waiting to get some money to go ahead with. We didn't get much up there, so then I finally started trucking. I bought a truck and started hauling cedar poles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had park changed very much between the time you left and the time you came back? Had many of the old timers gone?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah, most of them were gone. There were about three. There was one out that bought several places in there. What we call the Nolan Place, that was where this fellow, the widow was married the second time. He had the best place in the valley. He was one of the first ones. So when he sold, there were new people came and then there was another outfit came and bought that again. And they bought some other home status around there. So at one time, most of that was owned by two different outfits. There are still one or two in there, but not of the ones that were there when we lived there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you came back, was there still a real community there where people got together like they had in the older days?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No, there wasn't enough of them hardly. Did you know Charlie Gelliberg?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh yes, I talked to him.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, he was the oldest one in there and the only one left in there when we came back from Canada.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: One of my brothers was still there, but they moved out of there shortly after. You may know Norman Inger?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I know who he is

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: He was born over at Park, you know. And they were still there, but they moved out of there too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there any still getting togethers and that sort of thing with the new people?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No, not much. We were in there only about two years, I think it was. We moved out. No, there were just a few and they were new to us, you know.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: There wasn't much going on.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And then they consolidated with their school district, you know, so there was no school in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many of those people leave when they sold the timber to Potlatch?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Some of them did. There were some that had mostly timber, you know, so they didn't have much to stay there for. So some of them moved out. But we moved before that road was built in there, so we didn't know much. We weren't in contact with them much from then on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder if they got what the timber was worth from Potlatch?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No. No, they would sell for, I knew a place that sold for around fifteen hundred dollars, good timber on it. Potlatch was pretty shrewd about it. They'd send private men in there for them, you see, to buy the place. They'd come in and say, I would buy this place. Then they'd sell and then... But they finally found out so that they could get a little more. But it did happen to some people in Park. They sold, thinking they were selling to private... They were selling to you, you might say. Yeah. But there wasn't much of that because, and I don't know much about that because we moved away before there was much of that done.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I know one of our neighbors, he had a timber claim, you know, you can file on that. Quite a ways back in over towards what's called the Three Bear Country. And he got a little out of it, but I don't know exactly how much, but he didn't get much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know the Joe Wells?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh yeah. Up by Derry there. Yeah. That is, I knew about him and when we lived in Derry, when we put in that water system, one of the Wells...

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: What was his name? Chuck Wells?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah, he worked in there quite a while. They lived just each there and there they had a daughter, was a daughter and a son, went to school when Viola and my kids went to school there in Derry. So they were chumps. Yeah. They were decent people.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: When you left Park, where did you...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Pardon? When you left Park then, was that to move to Derry or to Helmer?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah, we stayed in Helmer one summer. I don't know just how long we stayed there now. It wasn't very long. Then we moved to Spring Valley. I rented a house there. That's just where Floyd started the school, one of my, next to Viola. We were close to a schoolhouse there and I did a little hauling around and I kept waiting for some money. I didn't get anything from there much, you know, so I couldn't decide just what to do.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So then from Spring Valley,

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: we... I got to study a little here. We moved to Derry. Then I began to work in town there in Derry then until we moved to Coeur d 'Alene.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you make money hauling poles then?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh yeah, but it didn't last long each year, you know. It was just a couple of months and then it'd get wet back in the mountains, you know, so you couldn't get in there. It was about three years I think I did that.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And then the last year that I was hauling poles, something happened to the market. I worked for the same man three years. He bought timber, you know, and had men to make the poles, get them up and then I'd haul them up. And that fall he came to me and he said, I can't have any poles next year, so I'm going to quit. Well, his brother started in then. He went over on Cedar Creek, you know where that is, where that road was in the park. Down in the canyon there he made up some poles. So I hauled that out and that was the end of it.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It sounds to me like it was rough, just rough for a lot of people to make a good living in those days.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: It was. It was. It helped a lot of them when that, what did they call it, that government...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The WPA?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So we moved to Coeur d 'Alene and I bought a track there and built and then I did a little carpenter work there. Then I went up to where they built that government training station up there, you know, Farragut. Worked there for a while. I got a lot of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you met your wife, did you know very, did you know her a long time before you decided to marry?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah, we were acquainted for five or six years.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: It was,

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: she was brought up quite religiously and you probably know about the Church of God religion. I don't know how it is now, but at that time they had the thought, you know, that they were the only church that was right. That anybody else was not, they were called unbelievers. And I was in that class.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And there is a place in the Bible that says, do not, I see how it does, do not...

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I can't get that word now. Anyway, it meant that she should not marry anyone that was not a member of that church.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: That was a rough one. Well, I was not particularly a member of any church at that time. And, but I wasn't an unbeliever exactly. I could go to any church and get a good thought. But, and we talked about it quite a lot. I talked with some ministers locally there. That was towards the last part I lived on Central Ridge. And they couldn't, there were certain questions in the Bible that I couldn't get straight and they couldn't help me. Even outside of the Church of God.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And she was hoping that I could join their church, you know. And we were ready to get married any minute if I would join. But you... But I couldn't, well, finally it went so far that there was a, there was a congregation, or you might say a convention of ministers of that church had a meeting in Spokane. And she and her mother were going up there. And she asked if I would go there. Well, I said I would. They went up there and then I got away so I could go and I went up there. I stayed there a few days. And of course I went to all the meetings there. She and her mother and I were there together.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And so, I think it was Eunice that talked to one of the ministers that might be able to help me. Eunice, her mother. That was my wife. Of course, she was not my wife then, but she was working to help along, you know. So one of them came in, I happened to be sitting in the chapel there. The minister of that church had his residence right connected with the chapel.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So Eunice and her mother, they stayed there and I had an apartment downtown, or a room downtown. I go up on the street car to this place that was out the way. And so he came in. And we sat down and talked. He didn't ask me to join the church or anything at the beginning because he hadn't seen me before. And so I started writing and asking a certain question. And the one that was the biggest problem, not with that church, but just about religion. Was about the creation. After God created heaven and earth and all the animals. And in the first chapter he created man and woman and he blessed them and showed them what they should have to eat and all. And then it says at the end of that, it says God saw what he had made and it was very good. Well, that sounded good. Then in the next chapter and the third chapter there was another creation. Well, the ministers that I had talked with said that's just a continuation of the first chapter. But it couldn't be because in the second chapter he just created a man. And it doesn't say he created it in God's image, he created a man. And then he built this altar and he put him in there. Then it says God said it isn't good for a man to be alone. I will make a help meet for you.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, hadn't God thought about that when he created the man? You see?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: That was part of it. So then he creates a woman. Then he puts him in there. And then he says don't eat off of that tree because if you do you're going to die. Then it seems God kind of faded out of the picture and then the serpent came there. And you know what went on there that they finally...

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, then the question in my mind was why did God let the serpent do this? Because another place in the Bible it says that God knows the past, present, and the future. He knows everything. He must have known what was going to happen there.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So I asked this minister, can you tell me why? And he sat there and looked at me a while and kind of studied and he said, you know, he said, it isn't necessary for the salvation of your soul to know those things. He might as well have said he didn't know. And then he looked at his watch and he said, I've got to go. That was all I got out of that convention.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, I didn't tell Eunice all of this, but I told her that I still couldn't see my way free. I said I've got to be honest with myself. I could have joined, sure I could have. I could have joined, we could have got married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would it be in the future for us and our children?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: You can't do that. You've got to be satisfied in your own conscience that you're doing the right thing.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And I told her, I said, so long as I can't get those answers, how can I believe it?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And I wouldn't say that I'm a saved man when I can't understand what I read in the Bible. There were other things too, but this was the main thing.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So, I had already sold my place over there and I was going to Canada.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And so to me it looked like we wouldn't get married. We couldn't. Well, she wouldn't give up. She said something will turn up. Yeah, but I said I'm going up there and you're going to be down here. Well, she still had faith, you know. She wouldn't give up. We said goodbye just like good friends and I went to Canada. And we corresponded and went on that first year. And it went on. I came back down here to see my brother and family and them. I went back to Canada.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Went on through the next year and we were corresponding. And I had given up. I wasn't thinking about marrying her at all. But still, I wasn't going to batche. I didn't want to batche, you know. And there was a couple of girls up there that would have wanted to get acquainted with me, but I just couldn't get interested at all. Not at all. They were decent people. I just couldn't. I just went on from day to day.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Finally, a letter came from her and she said, Or is there any other girl in your life?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I wrote back and said no. Went on a little while and she wrote up and she said, If you still want to marry me, if you come down, we'll get married. Just like that.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, I had to read that a few times, you know. So, I began to make plans right away. Well, Steiner had come up there at that time. And Simon, one of his brothers, came down.
